one_line_takeaway: LEGATO replaces full-model fine-tuning with lightweight, step-size-controllable Neural ODE adapters plus a trajectory-consistency regularizer for GAN identity unlearning, cutting fine-tuned parameters by 95% and update time by 67% while achieving lower FID (better retention) and comparable-or-better identity suppression than GUIDE and other repurposed baselines on FFHQ/CelebAHQ.
key_finding: On Table 1 (single-image setting unless noted), LEGATO achieves the lowest FIDpre in all three settings: 8.76 (Random) vs GUIDE 10.29 / DoCo 16.59 / LoRA 10.80; 6.12 (InD) vs GUIDE 7.77 / DoCo 12.23; 6.09 (OOD) vs GUIDE 7.44 / DoCo 11.19. LEGATO's ∆FIDreal is likewise lowest: 6.01 (Random), 1.05 (InD), 1.78 (OOD), reported by the authors as 14-29% (paper's abstract of the effect: OOD ∆FIDreal improves ~47% over next best) better retention than the next-best baseline. LEGATO's ID scores are competitive with or better than the strongest ID-suppression baselines (e.g., -0.07 Random, matching/edging out RG's 0.01 and GUIDE's 0.10; 0.00 for both InD and OOD, comparable to RG/SalUn). Table 2: full-fine-tuning baselines (GUIDE, DoCo, RG) update ~28.20M parameters at 3.6-4.9ms/epoch; LEGATO and the LoRA-style ablation update only 1.51M parameters (a 95% reduction), with LEGATO additionally achieving a 67% reduction in per-epoch update time (1.6ms) versus GUIDE, while still outperforming the LoRA-style baseline on retention. Table 3 (step-size sweep, CelebAHQ multi-image): Δt=0.4 is the empirical sweet spot (FIDpre 6.09, ∆FIDreal 1.78), degrading at both smaller (Δt=0.1: FIDpre 6.85) and larger (Δt=1.0: FIDpre 7.59, ∆FIDreal 3.22) step sizes, consistent with Theorem 2's predicted non-monotonicity. Table 4 (noise-attack robustness): LEGATO retains lower ID (0.00 vs GUIDE 0.02) and lower FIDpre (6.98 vs 8.11) than GUIDE after Gaussian latent-code perturbation. Table 5 (multi-identity unlearning, 2 and 3 identities on CelebAHQ): LEGATO shows markedly better ID/IDavg than GUIDE (e.g., 2-identity: ID -0.02 vs GUIDE's 0.26; IDavg 0.19 vs 0.42), supporting the conflict-free multi-identity claim (Theorem 3). Ablation (Table 6, CelebAHQ): adding the Neural ODE module alone improves FIDpre from 7.44 to 6.88 and ID from 0.02 to -0.02; adding TC on top further improves FIDpre to 6.09 and ∆FIDreal from 2.20 to 1.78, indicating TC's contribution is primarily to retention stability rather than forgetting strength. Tables 7-8: hidden dimension Chidden=256 and the Euler solver (vs RK4, midpoint) give the best retention/forgetting balance among tested configurations.
core_contribution: LEGATO reformulates generative identity unlearning (removing a specific person's identity from a pre-trained GAN, e.g. EG3D built on StyleGAN2) as a continuous-time transformation rather than a discrete fine-tuning step. Instead of fine-tuning the full generator, LEGATO inserts lightweight Neural ODE adapters after each synthesis-layer resolution stage while keeping the pre-trained generator weights frozen. Forgetting intensity is controlled explicitly via the ODE integration step size (forward-Euler discretization), and a 'trajectory consistency' (TC) loss term regularizes the ODE vector field (penalizing ||f(h(t+1),t+1,θ) - f(h(t),t,θ)||^2 across steps) to prevent 'catastrophic collapse' -- large, destabilizing degradation of the generator's ability to produce other (retained) identities during unlearning. The paper also proves three theorems: (1) the Neural ODE solution map is C1/smooth under Lipschitz + continuity assumptions on the vector field (standard Picard-Lindelof + Gronwall argument), (2) the forgetting-retention performance objective J(Δt)=F(Δt)+R(Δt) is non-monotonic in step size Δt with a non-empty optimal interval [Δt_min, Δt_max], and (3) under disjointness/locality assumptions on per-identity latent manifolds, ODE flows for distinct identities are non-intersecting and non-overlapping, giving 'conflict-free' multi-identity unlearning.
threat_model: The paper's own framing (from its Introduction) is privacy/right-to-be-forgotten: generative models trained on large face datasets can memorize and reproduce a specific real individual's identity (motivating citations: Carlini et al., USENIX Security '23, on training-data extraction from diffusion models; Lukas et al., IEEE S&P '23, on PII leakage in language models), which is undesirable for deepfake risk and conflicts with GDPR/CCPA erasure obligations. The unlearning goal is to make the generator unable to reconstruct a specific identity from its known latent code while preserving generation quality for all other (retained) identities. The one explicit adversarial evaluation is the 'Robust to Noise Attack' experiment (Table 4): Gaussian noise is added to the test latent codes of an already-'forgotten' identity to see whether the original identity can still be recovered/regenerated after this perturbation. This is a narrow robustness check on the unlearning method itself (does noise on the latent code re-expose the forgotten identity), not a broader adversarial threat model against the underlying generative model.
limitations: The paper itself does not include an explicit limitations section in the main text portion reviewed. Notable gaps visible from the text: (1) the method and all evaluation are restricted to a single GAN architecture (EG3D/StyleGAN2) and face-identity data (FFHQ, CelebAHQ); no diffusion-model or text-to-image unlearning results are shown despite diffusion models being discussed as motivating context, and despite the DoCo/SFD baselines the paper compares against being diffusion-model unlearning methods repurposed for this GAN setting. (2) The only adversarial/attack evaluation is a single Gaussian-noise perturbation on latent codes (Table 4); there is no evaluation against stronger recovery/re-identification attacks, fine-tuning-based relearning attacks, or membership-inference-style probing of whether the 'forgotten' identity's training influence is still extractable from model weights. (3) Baselines SalUn, RG, and DoCo were re-implemented by the authors themselves for this task (not the original authors' code for identity unlearning specifically), which the paper states but does not otherwise validate/discuss for fidelity. (4) The three theorems rely on idealized assumptions (global/local Lipschitz continuity of the learned vector field, disjoint compact per-identity manifolds, localized-unlearning vector-field modification) that are not verified to hold for the actual trained ODE adapter in practice -- they are provided as motivation/interpretation rather than certified guarantees tied to the empirical model.

datasets_used: Random (noise-sampled latent codes, no external dataset)
CelebAHQ (out-of-domain, single-image and multi-image identity evaluation)
quantitative_results: Table 1 ID: LEGATO -0.07 (Random) / 0.00 (InD) / 0.00 (OOD) vs GUIDE 0.10 / 0.06 / 0.02; IDavg (OOD multi-image) LEGATO 0.18 vs GUIDE 0.23
Table 2: fine-tuned parameters 28.20M (GUIDE/DoCo/RG) vs 1.51M for LoRA-style and LEGATO (-95%); parameter-update time 4.9ms (GUIDE) vs 1.6ms (LEGATO, -67%)
Table 3: optimal ODE step size ≈0.4 (FIDpre 6.09, ∆FIDreal 1.78) among tested {0.10, 0.20, 0.40, 0.60, 1.00}
Table 4 (Gaussian noise-attack robustness): LEGATO ID 0.00 / FIDpre 6.98 vs GUIDE ID 0.02 / FIDpre 8.11
Table 5 (multi-identity unlearning, CelebAHQ): 2-identity LEGATO ID -0.02 / IDavg 0.19 vs GUIDE-2nd ID 0.26 / IDavg 0.42; 3-identity LEGATO ID -0.02 / IDavg 0.20 vs GUIDE-3rd ID 0.28 / IDavg 0.47
Table 6 ablation (CelebAHQ): no-NODE/no-TC baseline FIDpre 7.44 -> +NODE only FIDpre 6.88 -> +NODE+TC FIDpre 6.09; ∆FIDreal 3.36 -> 2.20 -> 1.78
Table 7: hidden dim sweep {64,128,256,512}, best FIDpre at Chidden=256 (6.09)
Table 8: solver comparison, Euler FIDpre 6.09 vs RK4 6.21 vs midpoint 6.34
baselines_compared: GUIDE (Seo et al. 2024, prior generative identity-unlearning method)
SalUn (Fan et al. 2024, saliency-guided unlearning, re-implemented by authors for this task)
Restricted Gradient / RG (Ko et al. 2024, gradient-orthogonalization unlearning, re-implemented by authors)
DoCo (Wu et al. 2025, diffusion concept unlearning via adversarial training, re-implemented by authors)
LoRA-style discrete adapter (authors' own ablation baseline using low-rank adapters instead of Neural ODE adapters)
